data.title: Aptera Motors: Another Chance To Sell
data.description:
On Monday morning before the market opened, we received third quarter results from Aptera Motors (SEV). The company, which is looking to launch a solar-powered electric vehicle, is still far away from generating any actual revenues. With another quarter of losses and cash burn in the books, investors today are likely facing a massive amount of dilution moving forward as the company's financial picture remains quite troubled.

Previous coverage of the name

It was a little more than a month ago that I previously looked at Aptera, right after the company went public via a direct listing. At that time, I noted how the company was going to need a massive amount of capital to get its concept vehicle to actual production, with large losses and cash burn pressuring the balance sheet. Since that article, shares have lost more than 16%, while the S&P 500 has risen by almost 1.5%.

A look at the Q3 results

There is still no concrete timeline for when Aptera will start to generate any actual revenue. At the moment, the company is working on cost discipline and capital raising activities to support production validation. In Q3, the GAAP net loss was roughly $5.5 million, an improvement from the $8.2 million seen a year earlier. On an adjusted basis, the $2.9 million loss was basically half of the red amount seen in Q3 2024.

At the end of September, the company had roughly $12 million in cash and equivalents on its balance sheet, while being debt-free. That number was down about a million dollars from the end of Q2, as a few million in cash burn was offset by $2 million in grant funds received from the California Energy Commission. The company also has a $75 million equity credit line in place through which it can raise capital. In October, the company filed a registration statement for 6 million shares related to this share purchase agreement.

The outstanding share count is on the rise a bit currently. At the end of Q3, there were 9.28 million Class B shares outstanding, which are the ones that trade in the market. There were also another 18 million-plus Class A shares outstanding, which can be converted to Class B. As the company's 10-Q filing states, the Class B count as of November 14th was up to nearly 10.16 million. That number could rise quite substantially moving forward, between a combination of Class A conversions as well as equity sales to raise capital.

The current valuation picture

As of Wednesday's close, Aptera shares were trading at $6.69 each. If you combine the Class A and B outstanding share counts, you get a little more than 27.37 million total shares. That gives the company a current valuation of roughly $183 million at this point. Normally, I'd use price to sales to value the name, like I do with any electric vehicle makers that are consistent money losers, but the company hasn't generated any revenue yet.

EV leader Tesla (TSLA) goes for a low double-digit times its annual sales currently, while most other names go in the low single digits, well under 2 times their annual revenues. For Aptera to justify its current valuation, it needs to do quite a bit of revenue, and that's not likely coming until 2027 at the earliest. Because it just went public and is still rather small currently, there are no major Wall Street analysts covering the name yet.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Aptera reported its first set of quarterly results since going public on Monday, and the numbers were about as expected. The business is still several quarters away from generating any revenue, and management is working to keep operating expenses as low as possible for now to keep cash burn at a minimum. The company still needs to raise quite a bit of funding to get its solar electric vehicle to market, which means current investors will be diluted quite a bit moving forward.

With another quarter in the books and the balance sheet slightly weaker, I have to reiterate a sell on Aptera for the time being. With the company likely needing well over $100 million more before deliveries even start, the stock probably will face a ton of pressure as equity capital is raised. It would not surprise me if the outstanding share count more than doubles over the next year. Right now, there are so many electric vehicle companies out there, so even though Aptera is a bit different, it's got a long way to go to prove itself.

data.title: U.S. Managed Care Q3 2025 Earnings Recap: Costs Grow As ACA Worries Loom
data.description:
US managed care insurers' third quarter earnings season was characterized by higher medical costs amid political uncertainty surrounding extensions of Affordable Care Act tax credits.

Many of the top publicly traded US managed care insurers' medical costs grew during the third quarter, resulting in mixed responses from investors even as revenues grew year over year and earnings per share remained largely stable.

A common issue affecting the sector's third quarter earnings calls was ongoing cost pressure, primarily related to government-subsidized Medicaid and Medicare Advantage plans.

Costs still on the rise

After a challenging 2025 for industry leader UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH), the managed care insurer again highlighted the high costs associated with its Medicaid plans, which experienced heightened acuity levels since state-led procedural disenrollment resumed in March 2023 following the COVID-19 pandemic.

The path to Medicaid recovery will be challenging, as states have not funded the program in line with actual cost trends, according to comments on an Oct. 28 earnings call by Tim Noel, CEO of UnitedHealthcare, UnitedHealth's insurance arm.

"While we're making steady progress in bridging this gap with states, the mismatch between rate adequacy and member acuity will likely extend through 2026," Noel said.

Similarly, Molina Healthcare Inc. (MOH) CEO Joseph Zubretsky lamented a "very challenging medical cost environment" during an earnings call and said that Medicaid rates may not be adequate.

"Our early 2025 rate increases were sufficient at the beginning of the year, but as medical cost trends increased beyond those rates, our [medical cost ratio increased each quarter]," Zubretsky said. "The rate updates we received later in the year and risk corridors did not provide an adequate buffer."

Molina's Medicaid medical cost ratio reached 92% during the quarter, according to Zubretsky, while its senior-aimed Medicare plan hit 93.6% as high levels of utilization continued. However, even Molina's marketplace plans were not immune to rising costs, as plans purchased through state exchanges had a medical cost ratio of 95.6%, significantly higher than expected, according to the CEO.

Although Humana Inc. (HUM) maintained its full-year EPS estimate, CEO James Rechtin acknowledged during an earnings call that the company's Medicare Advantage Stars ratings were "disappointing."

"We are disappointed, but we are not surprised by our bonus year '27 Stars results," Rechtin said. "The results are consistent with our baseline planning scenario, and our outlook remains the same as we previously communicated at our investor conference in June."

Humana previously took issue with how the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services calculates its Star ratings, filing its first lawsuit against the body in October 2024, which was ultimately dismissed a year later. The government body annually issues quality ratings of Medicare Advantage offerings, which impact bonus payments insurers receive from the US government.

In Humana's current measurement year, bonus year 2028, the company is seeing meaningful year-over-year improvement across most metrics and is on the right track, Rechtin said.

ACA credits to lapse

Rate issues are not limited to Medicaid, as UnitedHealth joined a growing chorus of insurers expecting to be affected by changes in the Affordable Care Act markets.

ACA plans, which are offered through state-based marketplaces and directly from insurers rather than through an employer, have been under scrutiny as the extension of tax credits to lower plan costs has become a key issue for the recent federal government shutdown. While chatter remains of possible legislative solutions, several Democratic senators relented and ended the shutdown.

Prior to the shutdown, most insurers had already priced in the expectation that the tax credits would not be extended.

UnitedHealth's insurance arm submitted rate filings in nearly all of the 30 states where it offers ACA plans, reflecting an average rate increase of over 25%, according to Noel. Consequently, the company expects its ACA membership to drop significantly, Noel said.

"Where we are unable to reach agreement on sustainable rates, we are enacting targeted service area reductions," Noel said. "We believe these actions will establish a sustainable premium base, while likely reducing our ACA enrollment by approximately two-thirds."

Despite these challenges, analysts at J.P. Morgan ranked the insurer's stock "overweight," noting that the company expects to expand margins in 2026 through improvements in Medicare Advantage and commercial plans, which will offset the expected margin contraction in Medicaid.

Shares in UnitedHealth are down nearly 50% over the past year but rebounded some after Berkshire Hathaway revealed it had picked up about $1.57 billion in stock in the health insurer in the second quarter.

"We see this as not necessarily dissimilar from commentary in the second quarter; however, with the benefit of several more months of claims/trend progression, we think the incremental visibility underlines this positive commentary," the analysts wrote.

The ACA rate repricing may pose a more significant challenge for Centene Corp. (CNC), which has a disproportionately large number of members in the plans compared to its similarly sized competitors.

During a third quarter earnings call, Centene CEO Sarah London touted the benefits of extending ACA tax credits and that Centene believes "these tax credits offer critical support for hard-working Americans, small business owners and rural healthcare infrastructure."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Multiple Expansion Has Run Its Course, Upside Limited
data.description:
British American Tobacco (BTI) has been on a tear over the last two years. It's been a go-to stock for many investors since it provides a consistent dividend that's grown over the last two decades and has traded for a discounted valuation. It's also in an industry that has very predictable cash flows. These all made it a favorite amongst income investors who wanted a smooth return profile.

But more recently, I believe that it's been fully valued with little upside.

In this write-up, I want to dig deeper into

  • Why the stock has performed so well over the last two years, and why I think it's unlikely that it will continue
  • Growth prospects and risks in non-combustible products
  • Regulatory risks
  • Capital Allocation
  • What Investors Should Do

Past Performance

To understand where things will go, we have to step back and understand how we got here.

The valuation used to be a lot lower on a P/CF basis:

More recently, a high multiple has been realised. First off, British American Tobacco is a cash-generating machine. Contribution margins on its traditional product offering (combustibles) are wide. Even as the number of smokers has gone down over the long run, the pricing is such that they can raise prices to make up for lower volume, as these products are inelastic in demand. It's also a classic recurring model, as consumers will buy the same amount of the same product on a regular basis. Regulations make it so that barriers to entry are high.

There were also some major recent catalysts that allowed for multiple expansions. For a long time, the valuation was cheap, but there was a large debt overhang that prevented a significant return of capital to shareholders. In March 2024, the company announced it was selling a portion of its stake in ITC Limited (an Indian consumer goods company) for £1.57 billion. Soon after, it announced a share repurchase of £700 million, which would come from the proceeds of the sale. This catalyst was a major turning point since the company made it clear that it would reduce debt and return capital to shareholders, and if needed, do divestitures to make it happen.

The second major catalyst was in early 2025. British American Tobacco, amongst other companies, was in litigation in Canada, as it had long known about the risks associated with smoking and didn't adequately warn consumers:

March 7 (Reuters) - A Canadian court approved a plan to settle long-running tobacco lawsuits in the country as part of which the units of three Big Tobacco companies will pay C$32.5 billion ($22.67 billion), Philip Morris and British American Tobacco said on Friday.

The settlement resolves lawsuits — that have hung over Philip Morris (PM), British American Tobacco (BTI) and Japan Tobacco (OTCPK:JAPAY) (2914.T), — that claimed the Canadian units of the three tobacco giants knew since the 1950s their products were causing cancer and other illnesses and failed to warn consumers adequately.

The reason this was seen as a positive was that it removed an unquantifiable risk with a manageable, predictable long-term expense. The surprise from this ended up being a net credit, whereby the amount to be paid was lower than the liability provision that the company had been carrying on its books.

Beyond this, the macro backdrop also helped. Dollar-based investors often forget that British American Tobacco is a UK company that earns significant revenue in GBP. This makes it a boon when the dollar is weakening, as it has been over the last two years. The other thing to remember is that European developed market indices like the DAX or FTSE 100 have outperformed all major US indices. So, British American Tobacco, whose primary listing is as BAT on the London Stock Exchange, gets a tailwind.

I mention all this as it's unlikely to continue to repeat. The catalyst for debt reduction and buybacks is usually a one-and-done, as the market has fully priced it in now. Similar for the Canadian litigation risk. As for the weak dollar, it's possible it continues to weaken, but it's not a sure thing, and the same goes for European equities outperforming US equities. All to say, what made this rally have legs might not continue.

Growth Prospects

The company has set a goal for itself to have 50% of revenue come from non-combustibles by 2035. Although they have so far done well, the market for non-combustibles is far more competitive than their legacy products.

The key issues I see here are lower barriers to entry and a perpetual investment cycle, which causes uncertain unit economics.

When it comes to barriers to entry, British American Tobacco can't rely on its traditional moat: complex manufacturing, brand, and restrictive regulations. This forces them into a slim-margin, high-volume business model from their current wide-margin, low-volume model.

Firstly, British American Tobacco is facing competition from illicit products. The company, in its half-year report, said the following:

New Categories continued to grow, with revenue up 2.4% on a constant rates basis driven by Modern Oral (up 40.6%) and HP (up 3.1%). However, Vapour declined 13.0% due to the continued impact of illicit products in the U.S. and Canada and evolving market dynamics (in the UK and France).

The reason for this is that smoke-free products are far easier to produce than smoke products. Vapes require only small-scale electronic assembly and bulk sourcing of e-liquids (glycerin, propylene glycol, flavourings). Compare this to smoke products, which require high capital investment in specialised machinery and a more complex supply chain. This makes it so that the complexity of the product, which was previously part of the moat for British American Tobacco, isn't there anymore.

There's also independent data to back this up from the Truth Initiative (non-profit organisation), showing that a majority of e-cigarettes don't have an FDA marketing order:

This means that there will be a consistent supply of illicit vape products. My broader view of this is that it shows how easy it is to replicate non-combustible products compared to smoke products. This means that it's possible that the FDA cracks down on illicit vapes, which in the short term helps British American Tobacco, but long term, I think this is a sign that there will be more legal competition, as it's pretty easy to replicate e-cigarettes compared to smoke products.

The second key issue is the perpetual investment cycle. With its legacy product line, British American Tobacco can pretty much sell the same product again and again with no innovation. The clientele is hooked on the product the way it is and doesn't want any changes, hence no risk of rapid obsolescence. In comparison, with new smokeless products, they need to spend heavily on R&D along with marketing.

This can clearly be seen in their gross and contribution margins for combustibles vs new categories:

As can be seen above, the gross margin for combustibles is similar to new categories. But the contribution margin for new categories is only 10.6% whereas it's 57.4% for combustibles.

The investor presentation doesn't break down where all the extra expenses for the contribution margin come from, but we can take an educated guess that it's marketing and R&D. Since smoking advertisements are banned in most jurisdictions, there is no cost there for traditional products; it also provides a direct moat since new players can't come in and compete for that revenue, as those new players can't advertise, hence little ability to gain market share. In comparison, adverts for smokeless products are legal and more commonplace, and the clientele isn't sticky. As a consequence, large-scale spending on marketing is needed.

Combustibles don't have an R&D expense because the product is already fully mature. As for non-combustibles, they need constant updating in hardware (coils, heaters, batteries, chips), and regulatory requirements impose recurring spending on technical work. This all makes a material portion of R&D non-negotiable regulatory compliance.

These are key reasons as to why the contribution margin is lower. With a contribution margin of around a fifth of what it is for the legacy product line, that means that the goal of reaching 50% of revenue from non-combustibles by 2035 doesn't equate to much operating income-wise, as a vast majority of the operating income will still be attributable to combustibles, even if they hit that goal.

Regulatory Risks

I think the market is overlooking regulatory risks. Regulatory risks, by their nature, are long-term and cumbersome to analyse since it takes many years to get through the process of implementing them, and there are risks in the process if the regulatory guidelines are changed.

The most recent risk that British American Tobacco was able to dodge was the FDA's proposed ban on menthol cigarettes. In 2022, the FDA announced a proposal to prohibit menthol as a characterising flavour in cigarettes. After the FDA announces a proposal such as this, it goes through a process of feedback, revision, and final rule draft, then an internal legal review at the Office of Management and Budget (OMB). Only after all this is done is a new final rule implemented on the books. This is a multi-year process, and by the time it got to the point where the rule could be on the books, the 2024 election was near, and a choice was made to wait it out due to the political risks involved. In January 2025, the proposal was lifted under the Trump administration.

So in the short term, British American Tobacco dodged a bullet, but risks still remain. Future administrations could pursue this further, and many states on their own are already starting to. In 2020, Massachusetts banned the sale of all flavored tobacco, and a similar law was passed in 2022 in California.

British American Tobacco doesn't break down revenue to the point where we can figure out what the impact of a federal ban would directly be, but we can take an estimate based on knowing that 32% of all cigarettes sold in the US are menthol. Total combustibles revenue for H1 2025 was £4328 million, which would annualise to £8656 million. So, using the 32% figure, that's £2770 million attributable to menthol per annum. H1 2025 total revenue was £12069 million, which annualises to £24138 million. Meaning we can estimate 18% of total revenue is attributable to US menthol sales. I think the figure is higher since Newport, which is a product line of British American Tobacco, is the biggest menthol brand in the States, accounting for 35% of sales nationwide.

On the other hand, the silver lining is that the amount of revenue wouldn't likely go down by that much. Most smokers would switch to non-flavoured products, many of which are owned by British American Tobacco.

In addition to this, there are other regulatory risks playing out right now. The biggest one is an FDA-forced reduction in nicotine levels to non-addictive levels:

Today, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration issued a proposed rule that, if finalized, would make cigarettes and certain other combusted tobacco products minimally or nonaddictive by limiting the level of nicotine in those products. If finalized, the United States would be the first country globally to take such a bold, life-saving action to prevent and reduce smoking-related disease and death. The FDA first announced its intent to propose such a ruleExternal Link Disclaimer in 2018

This proposal came out in January 2025. This would still allow British American Tobacco to sell as usual, but require that nicotine levels be reduced to levels where they become non-addictive. In essence, this will eliminate the recurring nature of their business model. Beyond these risks, more minute risks exist, too. The two major ones are increased taxation (as a disincentive for consumers) and flavour bans on vapes.

What makes these regulatory risks difficult for investors is not their likelihood in any single given year, but their cumulative probability over a long horizon. The regulatory process is slow, political, and highly litigated, which creates the illusion of stability. But over a long time horizon, my belief is, the question isn't whether regulations tighten, but how much and how abruptly.

This is where I believe the market is overly complacent. British American Tobacco trades as if the worst-case regulatory scenarios have a negligible chance of occurring. Even if only one of them materialises, the impact could materially reduce volumes on the highest-margin products. And as I said in the previous section, the thin margins for new categories will make it difficult to replace a lot of revenue from the combustible product line.

The key takeaway of this section is that the market will demand a long-term lower multiple for the stock, as there has to be an embedded risk premium for these probable risks.

Capital Allocation

The capital allocation section ties in with the past two sections. We know that non-combustibles are thin-margin products without a sticky customer and that combustibles have long-term regulatory risks. Logically, the best thing would be to invest heavily in R&D to figure out how to widen margins on non-combustibles and create a repeat customer. Instead, the company seems to be doing share repurchases with more authorised:

In the near term, this will lead to an increased stock price. Because many shareholders are fixated on owner's yield (dividends + buybacks) and enjoy seeing a rising dividend along with a reduction in share count. But it comes at the expense of R&D for their new product lines.

Another major problem is their large debt load. Current total liabilities are over 6x TTM EBIT. So far, most of this debt has been from periods of time when rates were lower. But over time, the debt will have to be rolled over:

I will give credit where it's due and say that management did a good job in keeping maturities spread out. But nonetheless, over the next couple of years, a significant portion of debt will need to be rolled over at higher rates.

This debt also puts a drag on valuation. Earlier, I showed that the P/CF is 9.35, but if looked at on an EV basis, the EV/CF is around 15.4 at the time of writing. For a slow-growth company like British American Tobacco, I don't see much upside in terms of multiple expansion, as 15.4x is already pretty pricey.

Investor Playbook

When it comes to what investors should do, I believe it depends on what their objectives are. If you're an income investor just looking to clip coupons with minimal growth, thus satisfied with a high single-digit return, then I think a long position in British American Tobacco could still work. But most of us aren't just looking to collect some dividends with little stock price appreciation. As the name of this website implies, most of us are looking to generate significant alpha, and I don't believe British American Tobacco will continue to do that.

Quick summary of why I think upside is limited: revenue has been pretty stagnant for the last few years, even as the rate of smokers has gone down. This is because the industry as a whole has relied on raising prices to offset volume, which works, but only to a certain extent. There will be a point at which, even with inelastic demand, you can't continue to raise prices. The company knows this and henceforth looks to have 50% of revenue come from non-combustibles by 2035. The issue with non-combustibles is that with a contribution margin of 10.6%, versus 57.4% for combustibles, 50% of revenue won't make much of a difference, as it won't equate to anything near a 50% profit share. On top of that, non-combustibles require consistent R&D and have more competition. For these reasons, I'm not confident in the company's ability to generate long-term recurring revenue. The capital allocation doesn't add confidence either, with a focus on dividends and share repurchases over R&D, the latter of which I believe is a necessity to keep the company competitive in a post-combustibles world.

For me to consider a long position, I'd want a lower entry multiple (around 7x P/CF) where there's still probable multiple expansion. Along with that, significant progress on non-combustibles and a focus on debt reduction over buybacks. And a better tactical entry when the dollar is stronger and European equities are in correction territory against American equities.

For these reasons, I've put a hold rating on British American Tobacco.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Equity Residential: One Of America's Best Apartment REITs Is On Sale
data.description:
Equity Residential (EQR) is a quality company that has sold off because of short-term issues. This weakness has created a buying opportunity in a company that owns good assets, has a strong balance sheet, and pays a generous dividend that has grown over time. It's an attractive setup.

Introduction

Equity Residential has quietly grown into one of America's largest residential landlords. It owns and manages 318 properties spanning 86,320 apartments across various metros in the United States. The portfolio is mostly concentrated in coastal markets like Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Washington, D.C., but it is diversified among both coastal and interior metros.

Average rental rates are higher in markets like San Francisco, Washington, and New York, while markets like Atlanta, Denver, and Dallas are cheaper. The company owns high-quality apartments, so rents are generally higher than average rents in their respective communities.

Occupancy is a robust 96.3%, and it has stayed above 96% for at least the last year. Approximately 60% of tenants renew their lease each year. Rents have generally been flat over the last 12 months, but the company has had some success pushing through rental increases to renewing tenants.

Equity Residential rents class-A apartments to tenants with higher incomes than the median household. The majority of its tenants are either singles or couples. Its median tenant age is 33, and a typical renter has a rent-to-income ratio of just over 20%.

Like most residential REITs, EQR has a three-pronged approach it can use to increase earnings. It can:

  1. Bump rent to existing units
  2. Either acquire or develop new property
  3. Fix up current suites and then hike rents

Since the company's portfolio is fairly new, it mostly depends on increasing rents and acquiring new property. It will often recycle capital and sell older buildings, putting the proceeds into newer properties.

Why are shares so weak?

One reason why so many real estate investors are attracted to the residential sector is its perceived stability. People always need a place to live.

But the sector is a little more volatile than one might think. It is plagued by periods of overdevelopment and then underdevelopment as the pendulum swings the other way. Certain markets will have larger swings than others, influenced by factors such as the availability of land and the general affordability of development. In other words, some places are just easier to build in, and so they'll periodically suffer from oversupply.

That's exactly what's happening in many of Equity Residential's markets. Enticed by low-interest rates, population growth to Sunbelt markets, and affordable rents, developers in states like Texas, Georgia, and Colorado aggressively built new apartments. These new buildings started coming to market over the last 12-18 months, and this new supply has both pushed rents down and made it a little harder for landlords to attract new tenants.

Apartment starts have slowed considerably from the 2023 peak, but the damage has been done. It's going to take a little while to work through the glut.

Despite the new supply, the company is weathering the storm pretty well. It recently came out with Q3 results that saw funds from operations come in at $1.02 per share. That slightly beat analyst expectations of $1.01 per share, and was more than 4% higher than Q3 2024's number. That checked in at $0.98 per share.

The company pointed to strong results from San Francisco and New York as contributors to growth.

On the negative side, Equity Residential did cut its 2025 guidance ever so slightly. It originally projected FFO per share to grow by 8% in 2025. Guidance is now for FFO to grow by 6.4%. Revenue growth, net operating income, and earnings per share are all also projected to be weaker versus original estimates. The company also originally expected to acquire $1B worth of property in 2025, but that number has been ratcheted down to $750M.

In short, investors are nervous because they fear that more apartments will come online in 2026, and that will further impact Equity Residential's results.

Another issue is immigration. The Trump administration has taken a firm stand against illegal immigration, with the Department of Homeland Security recently saying that it has forcibly deported thousands of illegal immigrants from the country. An additional 2M more have left voluntarily. In addition, the department is also cracking down on visa abuse.

Although Equity Residential's apartments are generally more high-end and not usually home to illegal immigrants, the removal of so many people from its markets has an impact that can be felt throughout. The combination of more apartments and fewer people to live in them is not a great one for residential landlords.

The bull case

The bull case starts with an examination of how poorly shares have performed compared to Equity Residential's operating results.

As we saw above, the company cut guidance ever so slightly for the last quarter of 2025, but still expects to generate about $4 per share in FFO.

Despite very slight cuts in guidance, shares are down significantly. Over the last year, shares are down 20%. That seems like a rather large overreaction to me.

Management was also bullish during the latest conference call. CEO Mark Parrell reiterated that the company would see rental growth in 2025, although slightly less than first expected. The CEO also told investors that the company expects about 40,000 fewer new units in its markets versus 2025, which is a decline of about 35%. Lower supply plus continued strength from markets like San Francisco combine to paint a little rosier picture than before.

Management did not give much guidance towards 2026, which did disappoint some investors.

It's also much cheaper to rent versus own in many of Equity Residential's markets. On average, in the United States, the gap between owning and renting is strictly in the renter's favor. It's approximately $1,300 more per month to own property, rather than rent it. Mortgage rates have remained stubbornly high, and building materials continue to be expensive. This makes renting an easy choice, which ultimately bodes well for apartment owners.

Landlords also benefit from demographic trends. We're collectively waiting longer for major life events like getting married or having children, with many couples choosing to forgo the having kids part. That's bad news for society in general, but it's positive for an apartment REIT. Childless couples are more likely to be happy in an apartment.

Finally, I'll highlight the company's strong balance sheet, which is always a plus during uncertain times. The REIT has a debt-to-assets ratio of 28%, which is one of the lowest ratios you'll find in the sector. It's the equivalent of owing $140,000 against property that is worth $500,000, a position any landlord will confirm is pretty solid. The company is in no danger of breaching its debt covenants.

A great balance sheet ultimately gives a company options. EQR can use it to acquire new property or develop its own from scratch, which grows the business without having to issue new shares to do so.

Valuation

Equity Residential is a nice company that is currently trading for a reasonable valuation. You'll be able to find cheaper REITs out there, but its combination of growth potential, great balance sheet, and good assets is a unique one.

As it stands today, shares trade hands at $59.05 each. Analysts estimate the company will generate $3.99 per share in FFO in 2025, with that number increasing to $4.12 per share next year. That puts the stock at just over 14x forward FFO.

FFO is expected to grow steadily in 2027 and 2028, with analysts expecting that number to increase to $4.30 per share in 2027 and $4.46 per share in 2028. Steady growth is the story here, with just a little bit of caution mixed in.

Let me present another couple of metrics that suggest shares are cheap. The first is looking at where EQR traded five years ago. Shares are actually down about 5% in the last five years.

In 2020 the company earned $3.26 per share in normalized FFO. The company expects it will earn approximately $4 per share in normalized FFO in 2025. That's a 23% increase, yet shares are flat. This means that investors are getting 23% more earnings power per dollar versus five years ago.

I'll also highlight the dividend yield, which I believe is a quick and dirty way to determine value. If a company's yield is higher than usual, then it's often a good value. The opposite is often true if the yield is lower than normal.

Equity Residential is currently offering close to its best yield in the last five years, and the payout is a full 25% higher than the mean value over the last five years.

Dividend outlook

Speaking of Equity Residential's dividend, let's take a closer look at why this might be an attractive investment for the many Seeking Alpha readers who appreciate a good dividend.

First, the yield is attractive. The current dividend yield is 4.7%, which is significantly higher than most other S&P 500 constituents.

The payout ratio is reasonable too. The current annual dividend is $2.77 per share, while the company is expected to generate $4 per share in FFO in 2025. That gives us a payout ratio of approximately 70%, which is safe. I wouldn't lose too much sleep about the payout; it's secure.

I would argue that the payout will actually increase over time. The company has increased the payout by an average of 5.8% annually since 2011, showing investors it is serious about increasing the dividend.

Going forward, I would expect the dividend to increase by the same rate as earnings, or about 4-5% per year.

The bottom line

Equity Residential shares have fallen because investors fear a combination of new supply and decreased demand will hurt the business. We saw a bit of that so far in 2025, and the fear is it will persist through 2026.

But there are good things happening below the surface. The balance sheet is terrific. Apartment starts are steadily falling. Earnings are expected to increase. Dividend growth is solid. And the stock trades for a reasonable multiple.

We could see continued short-term pain, but over the long-term these are the kinds of boring assets I like to own. I also like getting paid nearly 5% while I wait for sentiment to change. My rating is a buy.

data.title: Weis Markets: The Pennsylvania Paradox
data.description:
Another quarter has passed, and there's nothing so exciting happening at Weis Markets (WMK) to justify adding it to your shopping cart.

Same old story here: demand holding steady, payout going nowhere.

And like I mentioned in my last write-up, they're running with a lot less cash (over 60% less than a year ago) not because they poured money into Capex or M&A, but because they scooped up shares from the Weis family trusts.

It sure doesn’t look like the smartest move for minority shareholders—and the market picked up on that pretty quickly.

The stock handed investors a return of roughly -13% since my first write-up, while the share price itself slid a little over -14%. That puts it way behind the S&P, which rose 5.8% over the same stretch, and even further behind other grocery names like Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY), Kroger (KR), and the rest of the pack.

So after all that, is Weis actually cheap?

Well, in my last article I said the stock was basically fairly valued after the Q2 drop. Since then it’s slipped a bit more, and now it’s sitting about 5% below the low end of my price target.

I think it’s worth checking the multiples at the end of the article and stacking them against other regional players like Ingles Markets (IMKTA) and Village Super Market (VLGEA).

That should give us a better feel for whether this ~6x EV/EBITDA (using the midpoint of my earlier $274 million EBITDA estimate) is actually a bargain or just looks like one at first glance.

A Peek at Q3

Before we jump into the numbers, it helps to get a sense of the tailwinds and headwinds in Pennsylvania and Maryland, which together account for roughly 80% of Weis’s footprint.

It's worth kicking things off by noting that, according to geolocation data, grocery store traffic beat both FSRs and QSRs every single month this year.

The gap even widened in September as consumers stayed price-sensitive. Of course, casual dining is holding up with prix fixe deals and solid value for the money, but overall the industry is still moving pretty slowly.

Just take a look at the traffic gap, and you will be able to see this:

The story gets even more interesting when we check out Weis’s traffic, which looks like it outpaced the rest of the industry in both markets.

Weis kept the second spot in Pennsylvania, with traffic up 3.7% in July, 5.8% in August, and 1.8% in September as more customers showed up. Aldi and Giant Eagle stayed ahead, while Giant Food Store remained on top but gave up a bit of share.

Maryland traffic slowed slightly, yet Weis still came out on top each quarter, adding 0.2% market share to reach 8.8%.

And what did that look like in the numbers?

For starters, revenue was up 4.4% from last year, landing at $1.24 billion—a level we hadn’t seen in some time.

Breaking it down, most of the money still comes from groceries, 80.2% of the total (growing 2.4% year over year). If you look back a bit, you can see that slice slowly shrinking as the pharmacy keeps bringing in more of the revenue.

And this is much more common than you think.

Loblaw (L:CA), Canada’s biggest retailer, has Shoppers Drug Mart pulling a lot of weight, and even Poland’s Dino Polska (OTCPK:DNOPY) runs eZebra.

In the case of Weis (I don't think I mentioned this in the last write-ups), they run the so-called Weis Pharmacy as a store-in-store concept, taking advantage of the existing terrain.

Pharmacy sales jumped 14.9% from last year, reaching $178.3 million in the quarter (14.5% of total revenue), and they’re still one of the biggest forces pushing the company forward.

In addition to the new stores they opened last year (at least half a dozen, including the conversion of a Sunnyway Foods in Pennsylvania at the end of last year), they reported growth in same-store sales.

At this point, you should have already guessed this from the traffic data I gave you, right?

Year-over-year growth stuck at 2.5%, just like last time. Though counting fuel, they fall short by about 50 bp. Over the past 32 weeks, same-store sales have increased by just 1.7% year-over-year, about 30 bp shy of what we saw in the same stretch last year.

Since traffic keeps moving up, what we’re likely seeing is shoppers trimming their basket size and/or leaning harder into the famous private-label items.

And just look at the cost side, and it's pretty clear why.

While occupancy costs remained virtually flat year-over-year, Weis saw its margin squeezed by COGS (+4.7%) and Labor (+5.3%), compressing operating profit from $29.5 million last year to $22.3 million.

In other words, the operating margin slipped from 2.5% to 1.8% this year. That's a pretty big slide.

Here's where the scale advantage becomes obvious.

Stack their margins next to the big players (and their private-label power), and the difference appears. Let's compare the EBIT margin that each one pulled out of their hat this year:

But you know what really worries me when I go through these numbers?

Roughly 60% of the chain sits in the Pennsylvania area, where wages still follow the federal floor of $7.25 an hour. In June, the General Assembly approved a plan to lift that to $15 in the 20 largest counties and $12 in the smallest ones.

Lancaster is likely in that first group, and because it’s Weis’s strongest market, higher labor costs would put even more pressure on margins that are already pretty tight.

At the moment, the minimum wage there remains at $7.25, but there are strong chances that it will increase next year.

With margins that weak, you’d expect FCF to look pretty flat. It did, but not quite as much as you’d think.

In fact, if you look only at OCF, you’ll notice it jumped ~130% year-over-year to $59.2 million. However, if we look only at adjusted OCF (without accounting for changes in operating assets), we see a drop of 8.3% to around $50 million.

This time around, Capex jumped to $63.9 million, mainly because of the remodel push and the opening of three new stores, including the one in Charlotte Hall, MD.

Naturally, thinner margins plus a jump in Capex left FCF pretty dry. They still have $177.6 million in cash and short-term investments, but that’s nowhere near the $337.9 million they had on hand before that insider buyback last quarter.

When you look at FCF, it's clear Weis isn't delivering what you'd hope for in a Defensive Pick.

In the past two years, they only covered dividends with FCF a couple of times and had to tap into their own cash more than once to handle Capex. All of this keeps Weis looking like an underdog to me.

Reviewing Some Scenarios

Like I've been saying, the margin squeeze is real, and at this pace we might see EBITDA margins slip below the old normals in FY 2025 and FY 2026.

That said, I want to lay out a few scenarios so we can rethink the price target:

  • Base: EBITDA margin at 4.9%, in line with recent quarters. Here I am not considering a return to the 5-year average as I had previously projected;
  • Bull: EBITDA margin slightly below the 5-year average, already considering pressure on labor costs and COGS, at around 5.2%;
  • Bear: Cost pressures continue and are even being exacerbated by the increase in the minimum wage in Pennsylvania. Here, I imagine it could shrink to 3.5%.

If we aim for something like $4.9 to $4.95 billion in revenue for all of FY 2025, and we stack Weis up against smaller regional names like Ingles and Village (average EV/EBITDA of 6.7x), we end up with the following price targets:

If we go with the base case, Weis is basically priced about where it should be.

I’m sticking with my ‘Hold’ rating here—Pennsylvania headaches and COGS pressure aside—mostly because there are a few things in the mix that still give me a bit of confidence.

The first one, and honestly the thing that would've pushed my rating the other way if it weren't happening, is that Weis is actually beating the market in both Pennsylvania and Maryland.

Pharmacy sales should keep acting as a nice buffer if grocery starts to cool off, even in one of those tighter-margin stretches where every little bit counts.

With traffic holding up this well, I can see the remodeling push (the same one that sent Capex through the roof) paying off with better returns in FY 2026 and FY 2027.

Basically, FCF would return to stable levels, and dividends would once again be well protected.

All in all, in my base case, Weis still behaves like a bond proxy, but now with a little more risk in the mix. It’s something worth hanging on to if you already own it, but I still wouldn’t be adding to the position right now.

data.title: TUR: Positive Catalysts, Expensive Valuation
data.description:
The Turkish economy has been on a general positive trend so far this year, with GDP growth of 4.8% on an annual basis for the second quarter of 2025; meanwhile, the iShares MSCI Turkey ETF (TUR) which aims to provide exposure to the country, has been negatively performing for some time now, falling around 8% year to date.

Interestingly, I wanted to learn more about this disconnect and give my view on this ETF. So let's go.

The Fund

The iShares MSCI Turkey ETF aims to provide exposure to the broad-based Turkish equity market. The ETF passively tracks the benchmark MSCI Turkey IMI25/50, whose main goal is to measure the performance of the Turkish equity market, with no apparent cap focus, providing exposure to large-, mid- and small-cap companies. The index covers 99% of the total free float adjusted market cap, which literally means you are getting exposure to most of the market you can possibly get. The median constituent market cap is $439 million. The 25/50 requirement for Regulated Investment Companies (RICS) means that the maximum value of 25% of the fund can be invested in one issuer and that the sum of all issuers above 5% cannot be above 50%.

The weighting is market cap, and the majority of the fund is comprised of mid-cap stocks- 49% with market cap ranging between $2 and $10 billion, around 25% in small cap stocks with market cap below $2 billion, and around 12% in large-cap stocks with market cap above $10 billion. The total number of constituents is 83. The expense ratio is 0.59%, and together with the bid/ask % spread of 0.15%, it earns a Seeking Alpha grade of C. Liquidity is high from a retail investor point of view, with an AUM of around $168 million and an average daily dollar volume on a 3-month basis of $5.2 million, earning itself a B- Seeking Alpha grade. The top 10 holdings constitute around 49% of the total fund assets, and the fund has a standard deviation of around 17%. The fund as a whole has a total Seeking Alpha Risk grade of F.

The Industrials sector is a dominant player in the capital markets in Turkey in terms of traded companies, and it represents around 29% of the ETF holdings, followed by the Financials sector with around 20% and the Consumer Defensive sector with around 13%, as we can see below:

The fund is a flagship fund offering exposure to the Turkish economy, and to my knowledge there is no other fund offering comparable exposure opportunities.

The fund has below-average Seeking Alpha grades, with the average grade being C. Relative to the past 6 months, the fund has improved its Dividends grade, while downgrading its Momentum grade in the past 3 months.

The Economy

The economy of Turkey has been surprisingly resilient this year, beating forecasts with 4.8% GDP growth in the second quarter, above the market consensus of 4.1% year-over year growth. The better-than-expected results were majorly driven by the private consumption and the construction boom, with construction investments rising around 11% year over year. What is a positive for this ETF, as around a third of it are industrial companies? The pickup in growth in that sector. Industrials contributed around 1.2 pp, recovering after 3 quarters of negative impact. As stated by ING, and I generally share their opinion, the current macroeconomic outlook is positive, as the economy is experiencing a positive momentum in growth driven by investments, industrial activity, and ongoing monetary easing.

I have to mention that even though inflation is on a downward trend, it is still relatively high, and a pickup in inflation could certainly hamper macroeconomic prospects.

The Industrials and Financials Sectors

As I outlined earlier, the two biggest drivers of the economy and this ETF are the Industrials and Financials sector and we will focus on them as I believe most of the ETF performance will be impacted by the policy rate of the Turkish Central Bank and the strength of the Turkish manufacturing sector.

Starting off with industrials, the economy has been largely boosted by the manufacturing sector as of late, as industrial production has been growing in high single digits in the summer months and 2.9% year over year in September. Naturally, Turkey, being a major hub for global manufacturing, is of course impacted by global demand and its biggest trading partners. The list is a diversified one, ranging from China to EU countries and the US. According to the IMF World Economic Outlook updated in July 2025, these economies are expected to either pick up growth or slightly decelerate, and this is a positive catalyst for Turkish manufacturing. Another positive catalyst is the ongoing monetary easing cycle; for example, Turkey's biggest industrial company, Tupras, issues floating rate notes connected to the TLREF, which is abnormally high relative to funding in western countries. A continuation of the easing cycle means lower borrowing costs and, hence, larger profits for these companies.

The Financials sector on the other hand, is inversely correlated to this change. The Turkish banks' profitability has been negatively impacted by the cutting cycle so far, as it has diminished net interest margins and lowered industry profits. The negative change in net interest margin is happening because liabilities such as deposits reprice much faster than assets such as loans. Well, according to Goldman Sachs, further interest rate cuts could ease this mismatch and nudge the banks toward higher profitability margins in the period ahead. According to Goldman Sachs analysts, they project a drop in the policy rate to 33%, and with that, they project a recovery in net interest margins and profitability peaking in mid-2026. This is also a positive catalyst for the ETF.

Naturally, we continue with the valuation, and we pose the question, are we paying a good price for these catalysts?

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, the fund looks overvalued. With a P/E ratio of 13.07x, the fund is around 5 standard deviations above its 10-year average of 6.4x.

From a technical perspective, the fund is facing a downward trajectory as it is below the 20D, 50D and 200D MA:

Risks

Key risks to consider and would impact my thesis are obviously currency volatility, as the lira remains vulnerable to swings in its value and has been depreciating for some time now, and further depreciation is a key risk for the Turkish economy. Connected to this issue, or preceding it, is inflation. As I already mentioned, inflation has been in a downward spiral the past year, but it is still present and at a high plateau and could impact consumer spending and the economy as a whole. Political uncertainty, global trade tensions, and slowing global growth are also risks to monitor.

Conclusion

The Turkish economy has been performing better than expected so far this year, while the equity market has been lagging. The iShares MSCI Turkey ETF presents an adequate investment vehicle to get exposure to this market, as it provides exposure to the broad Turkish capital market. Key catalysts include the ongoing monetary easing cycle lowering financing costs for the industrials sector and improving the net interest margin mismatch for banks; however, when we take into account the relatively expensive valuation of the fund relative to its history, I would currently rate the fund as Hold.

data.title: Vishay Intertechnology: The Bottom May Not Be In With The Downtrend Shown To Be Intact
data.description:
Vishay Intertechnology (VSH), a manufacturer of discrete semiconductors and passive electronic components, did more than just release its latest earnings report on November 5. VSH fell and broke below a trendline, which had been in place since the stock started its rebound following the multi-year low in April. This was significant because, up to that point, it could be argued the bottom was in, with the stock moving higher on the back of this trendline. This argument has fallen apart with the latest developments since the bottom may not yet be in, especially when adding the latest earnings results to the mix, along with other developments. Why will it be covered next?

VSH loses support

A previous article from last August took note of how the stock fell off a cliff in August after the Q2 FY2025 report. This caused VSH to lose a portion of what it gained thanks to the rebound in the stock following the multi-year low in April. This rebound still appeared to be in place, but there was reason for caution in light of the quarterly results, which included missing earnings expectations by a wide margin. Accordingly, I chose to rate VSH a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the stock recovered from the selloff following the Q2 FY2025 report to move higher. In fact, for more than half a year, VSH showed positive momentum following the April low. If, for instance, the lows during this period are connected, a trendline appears, which is ascending due to the lows moving higher.

On the other hand, VSH also appeared to have problems getting past the $17-18 price region. The stock made several attempts, but it was unable to get past the $17-18 region in July, which happened again in October, as shown in the chart. Both instances were followed by the stock turning south after failing to move higher. Still, as long as support was moving higher along with the aforementioned ascending trendline, there was reason to be optimistic that the stock would eventually break higher on a future attempt.

However, this changed after the selloff following the release of the Q3 FY2025 report in November, because it caused the stock to break below the trendline, which means support has been lost. The stock was in position to move higher prior to this, but the latest developments have opened up another possibility, which is that the stock has not seen the bottom, at least not yet.

Why VSH may have to go lower

VSH hit a multi-year low of $10.35 in April 2025, which was mainly the result of the tariff turmoil in the stock market. This low was in turn the culmination of a decline in the stock that started with a high of $30.10 in July 2023. If we then assume the rally following the April low of $10.35 was a retracement of this move downwards, then it is worth noting how the recent difficulties of the stock in terms of moving higher occurred close to the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of $30.10 to $10.35, which is $17.89.

This is within the same $17-18 region, which VSH was unable to cross, as mentioned earlier. In fact, the highest the stock was able to get following the April low was in July with a high of $17.88, or $0.01 below $17.89. If the recent rally in the stock following the April low was thus nothing more than a retracement of an earlier decline, which halted at a Fibonacci level, then the possibility arises that the April low was not the bottom it may have appeared to be.

The actual bottom is yet to be determined in this case. Furthermore, a look at the chart below suggests VSH is still in a downtrend, in spite of the rebound in the stock following the April low. It's also worth noting how the stock reacted once it reached the descending trendline in October. VSH reversed course to head south, which is reminiscent of what happened before.

Not only was the stock unable to get past the trendline on three separate occasions, but they were each followed by a major decline in the stock of roughly equal size. For instance, the stock fell from roughly $30 to $21 in July 2023, and the stock fell from roughly $24 to $15 in July 2024. Both of these selloffs happened after the stock reached the trendline.

It is not guaranteed, but if this pattern holds, then VSH could be due for a similar selloff after the stock reached the same trendline in October. This suggests that even with all the ground lost in November and the stock in oversold territory, the stock could have further to go down, potentially bringing the stock down to the April low of $10.35 for what could be a retest, and it may even go lower.

What was in the Q3 FY2025 report?

VSH could have used a good Q3 FY2025 report, especially since the preceding, or Q2 FY2025, report caused a double-digit drop in the stock price after earnings came in way below expectations. As it turned out, VSH reported non-GAAP EPS of $0.04 on revenue of $790.6M in Q3 FY2025. In comparison, VSH earned $0.08 on revenue of $735.4M in Q3 FY2024.

These results were in line with expectations, unlike the preceding quarter, which fell short of expectations, but the Q3 FY2025 report still disappointed like the Q2 FY2025 report, except in another way. Book-to-bill fell below one for the first time this year, unlike the expansion seen in the preceding quarters, which suggests demand is faltering. In terms of GAAP, VSH posted a net loss of $7.9M, or $0.06 a share, and in terms of adjusted EBITDA, VSH earned $76.0M with a margin of 9.6%.

VSH finished the quarter with total debt of $1,042M and net debt of $597.9M, partially offset by cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $444.1M. The latter is less than the $590.29M at the start of the fiscal, which can be attributed to the ongoing expansion of production capacity as part of “Vishay 3.0” and free cash flow falling into the red due to the required capex spending. In Q3 FY2025, free cash flow was negative $24.3M, more than negative $8.8M of one year ago. YTD, free cash flow reached negative $218.3M.

Q4 FY2025 guidance calls for revenue of $770-810M and a gross margin of 19.5%, plus or minus 50 basis points, or the same as in Q3 FY2025. Using these guidelines, I estimate Q4 FY2025 will come in similar to Q3 FY2025 with non-GAAP EPS of $0.04 on revenue of $790M. FY2025 is projected to end with a non-GAAP loss of $0.02 on revenue of $3,058.1M with the latest quarterly guidance.

In comparison, VSH earned $0.48 on revenue of $2,937.6M in FY2024. FY2025 adjusted EBITDA is projected to end up at $269.97M since adjusted EBITDA totaled $193.97M in Q1-Q3 FY2025 and assuming Q4 FY2025 adjusted EBITDA is similar to the preceding Q2 FY2025, or $76M. This is also below the adjusted EBITDA of $317.4M in FY2024.

FY2025 is very likely to end up as the second consecutive year VSH fails to hit the targets set out in the current financial model, including growing revenue at a CAGR of 9-11%, which covers the period FY2023-2028. On the contrary, revenue has yet to go above FY2023 revenue of $3,497M, the base, let alone grow at a CAGR of 9-11%. In other words, VSH is very much in a downturn.

Why valuations could force the stock price lower

The balance sheet got worse, and that is expected to continue with free cash flow expected to remain in negative territory due to continued capex spending. This is in part reflected in the relatively low valuations VSH fetches. For instance, VSH has a book value of $2,083.07M with total assets of $4,195.98M and total liabilities of $2,112.91M, which translates to a book value of $15.36 per share since the total number of outstanding shares is 135,588K.

The stock closed at $13.24 on November 14, which means VSH is valued at less than its book value of $15.36 a share with a price-to-book of 0.86x. Some may see a stock trading at less than book value as an undervalued stock, but in the case of VSH, this reflects the future decrease in book value with free cash flow in the red.

In fact, VSH trades at multiples that support the notion the stock needs to trade lower, on top of what the charts are pointing to. For instance, VSH has an enterprise value of $2,392.9M with a market cap of $1,795M and net debt of $597.9M. If we use the earlier estimate of $269.97M for FY2025 adjusted EBITDA, then EV/EBITDA is 8.9x. This is significantly higher than the 6.04x VSH has traded for on average in the past five years. If VSH were to trade in line with the average, then enterprise value would need to fall to $1,630.6M or a market cap of $1,032.7M, which would push the stock price below the April low.

Another methodology, like the one espoused by Peter Lynch, also suggests the stock can trade lower. According to this methodology, a stock ought to have a PEG equal to one to be considered fairly valued relative to growth. This means that with an EV/EBITDA of 8.9x, EBITDA needs to grow at a CAGR of 8.9% in the next 3-5 years for a PEG of one. Yet VSH has not been able to reach this growth rate in the last 3, 5, or even 10 years, which means there is reason to doubt VSH can hit the desired growth rate. If EBITDA grows at a slower pace than 8.9%, then the stock price needs to go lower.

Investor takeaways

VSH has lost 17.8% of its market cap since the Q3 FY2025 report was released. Book-to-bill fell below one, and while it is possible for it to get back above one, it points to soft demand, something that VSH has struggled with since 2023. It also comes at a time when VSH continues to proceed with planned production capacity expansion.

This requires capex spending, which is why free cash flow is in the red, something that has negatively affected the balance sheet and is likely to continue to do so. Depreciation is also going up, which will affect the income statement. If demand does not get better and it remains soft, VSH could come to regret expanding production capacity. Soft demand and expanded production capacity don’t combine well.

More significantly, the stock move has opened the door to a possible retest of the multi-year low in April because it pushed the stock below a trendline that had been in place following the low in April. It also suggests that the April low was perhaps not the bottom, as could be argued prior to this, but that the decline in the stock since the July 2023 high is still ongoing.

While VSH is available at what may appear to be low multiples at first, they are not low enough to rule out the stock from going lower. For instance, VSH is valued at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.86x, which may draw interest, but this is a reflection of the ongoing deterioration in the balance sheet, the result of negative free cash flow from capex spending and continued net losses in terms of GAAP. In other words, the charts and multiples seem to agree the stock needs to trade lower, possibly towards the April low of $10.35.

I am therefore neutral on VSH with everything earlier in mind. It’s possible for there to be a bounce in the stock since it is oversold, but if the long-term trend in the chart is anything to go by, on top of the state of the income statement and the balance sheet, and notwithstanding the sizable reduction in the stock price in recent days, VSH is likely to available at even lower prices in the near future.
